shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the meadow in motion: grass waving, wings crossing, everything moving

segments.text: For fourteen episodes, this series has chased living things. But the ground they all stand on was here first — and it is worth asking what this world looks like to the only residents that were never alive at all.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a boulder fills the frame, absolutely still; grass whips around it

segments.text: While the garden hurries, these wait. And every one of them is a page of the planet's history, written in mineral.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a slow, reverent circle around a single weathered boulder

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: macro across the surface: crystals, grain, and glassy facets in the light

segments.text: Every rock in this garden belongs to one of three families, sorted by how it was born. The first is igneous — stone born of fire. Molten rock, cooled and frozen solid: fast at the surface as lava, into fine dark stone; slow underground as magma, growing the big glittering crystals of granite.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a stone showing fine horizontal layers, like the pages of a book

segments.text: The second is sedimentary — stone built in layers. Sand, mud, and grit settle, pile up, and are pressed together over ages into stone you can read like a stack of pages. Sandstone, limestone. These are the rocks that trap fossils — the only stone that keeps a record of the life that settled into it.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a stone with warped, banded streaks frozen through it

segments.text: And the third is metamorphic — stone transformed. Take any rock, bury it deep, cook it under heat and crushing pressure, and it changes into something new without ever melting. Limestone becomes marble; mud becomes slate. The banding you see is the mark of that ordeal, frozen in place.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: rise above the meadow to take in hills, slopes, and scattered stone

segments.text: And no rock is ever finished. All three kinds are stops on a single slow loop the whole planet runs. Deep rock is lifted into mountains. Mountains are worn down to sediment. Sediment is buried, pressed, and heated back into stone — or driven deep enough to melt and rise again as magma.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a boulder's surface, cracked and flaking, grains loosening at the edge

segments.text: The rock cycle. It has no beginning and no end, and it turns on a clock so slow that a human lifetime cannot see the hand move. Every stone in this garden is somewhere on that loop, mid-journey, whether it looks it or not.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: rain streams down the stone face; ice glinting in a crack

segments.text: Which brings us to the quiet work these boulders are doing right now — breaking down. It is called weathering, and it comes two ways. The physical way is brute force: water seeps into a crack, freezes, expands, and levers the crack wider — freeze after freeze, until the stone splits.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: a patch of gray-green lichen spreading across bare stone

segments.text: The chemical way is subtler. Rain is faintly acidic, and over time it eats at the minerals themselves, softening the stone from within. And look here — lichen, the pioneer. Lichens colonize bare rock where nothing else can live, and as they grow they help pry mineral grains loose, beginning the work.

shot.subject: worm
shot.notes: below: a thin skin of dark soil where crumbled rock meets grass roots

segments.text: Grind stone fine enough, mix in the remains of the living, and you have made the one thing every plant in this series depends on: soil. Every blade of grass, every flower, every tree is rooted in weathered rock. The mountains, patiently, are becoming the meadow.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: macro on the stone's surface, mineral grains catching the light

segments.text: And as the rock breaks down, it pays out. Locked inside stone are the mineral nutrients that plants cannot make for themselves — calcium, potassium, and more. Weathering releases them slowly into the soil, a trickle of fertilizer measured out over centuries.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: grass and flowers growing thickest in the soil around a cluster of boulders

segments.text: So the stillest things in the garden are quietly feeding the busiest. The stone does not grow, does not move, does not care — and yet the whole living field is drawing, grain by grain, on an account these boulders have held since before the first seed.

shot.subject: ant
shot.notes: an ant crosses a rock face and slips into a shaded crack

segments.text: Stone gives shelter, too. A boulder's cracks and hollows are microhabitats — cool shade in the heat, a windbreak in the storm, a hiding place from anything hunting in the open. To an ant, a rock is not an obstacle. It is a whole landscape of addresses.

shot.subject: rock
shot.notes: evening: a boulder radiating the day's warmth, small life gathered at its base

segments.text: And stone keeps time with the sun. All day a rock banks heat into its mass; all evening it pays that heat back out — a warm hearth in the cooling meadow that the cold-bodied know by address. The thing that seems most dead in the garden runs its only heating system.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: rise high above the meadow until hills, shores, and hollows read as one sculpture

segments.text: Stone is the slow foundation everything else is built on — soil, shelter, warmth, and the mineral wealth of the whole living field. But the rocks rest on a larger stillness yet: the ground itself, with its raised hills and drowned hollows. And that ground, unlike any wild country, remembers being shaped.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the whole world from above: every contour a deliberate stroke

segments.text: Season three begins with the deepest story in the garden: the land, and the hand that sculpted it. Next: the Land.
